questions.text: Which statement best describes a fundamental principle of the cell theory?
options: All living organisms are composed of one or more cells
All cells contain a membrane-bound nucleus
Cells can be created from non-living materials
All cells are capable of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Cell theory states that all living organisms are composed of cells, cells are the basic unit of life, and all cells arise from pre-existing cells. Not all cells have a nucleus (prokaryotes lack one).

questions.text: Which scientist is credited with first observing and naming "cells" after examining cork under a microscope?
questions.explanation: In 1665, Robert Hooke observed cork under a microscope and named the box-like structures "cells" because they reminded him of monastery rooms. Leeuwenhoek later observed living cells.

questions.text: Which component is NOT part of the modern cell theory?
options: Cells can spontaneously generate from non-living matter
All living things are made of cells
Cells are the basic unit of structure and function in living things
All cells come from pre-existing cells
questions.explanation: The modern cell theory does not include spontaneous generation. This idea was disproven by experiments by Pasteur and others, showing that cells only arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.text: A scientist examines two different cell types under a microscope. Cell A has a defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. Cell B lacks a nucleus and has no membrane-bound organelles. Which conclusion is correct?
options: Cell A is a eukaryote and Cell B is a prokaryote
Cell A is a prokaryote and Cell B is a eukaryote
Both cells are eukaryotic with different evolutionary stages
Both cells are prokaryotic with different sizes
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles, while prokaryotic cells (bacteria and archaea) lack these features. The presence or absence of a nucleus is the key distinguishing feature.

questions.text: An experiment shows that a certain type of cell cannot be grown in culture unless it is placed in contact with other cells of the same type. This observation best supports which principle?
options: Cells arise only from pre-existing cells
Cells can only survive in groups
All cells are identical in structure
questions.explanation: This observation supports the principle that cells come from pre-existing cells. Some cell types require signals from neighboring cells to divide and survive, demonstrating cell-to-cell communication and dependence.

questions.text: Scientists discovered cells in the 1600s, but cell theory was not fully developed until the 1800s. Which technological advancement was most critical for completing cell theory?
options: Improved microscope lenses that allowed observation of cell division and reproduction
The invention of the electron microscope
questions.explanation: Better microscopes in the 1800s allowed scientists like Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow to observe cell division and establish that all cells come from pre-existing cells. This completed cell theory before electron microscopes existed.

questions.text: Which organelle is the site of protein synthesis in a cell?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the cellular structures where proteins are synthesized. They translate messenger RNA (mRNA) into polypeptide chains that fold into functional proteins.

questions.text: Which organelle contains the cell's genetic material (DNA)?
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control center of eukaryotic cells, containing the cell's DNA and directing cellular activities including growth, metabolism, and reproduction.

questions.text: Which organelle is responsible for producing the majority of ATP through cellular respiration?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the "powerhouses" of the cell, generating ATP through aerobic cellular respiration. They have their own DNA and are believed to have originated from ancient bacteria.

questions.text: Which structure is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a rigid cell wall for structural support and chloroplasts for photosynthesis. Animal cells lack both of these structures, though both cell types have nuclei, mitochondria, and ribosomes.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the cell membrane?
options: To control what enters and exits the cell
To produce energy for the cell
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier that controls the movement of substances in and out of the cell. It is composed of a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins.

questions.text: A cell is observed to have a high concentration of rough endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus. What is the most likely function of this cell?
questions.explanation: Rough ER (studded with ribosomes) synthesizes proteins, and the Golgi apparatus modifies, sorts, and packages proteins for secretion. Cells with abundant rough ER and Golgi are typically gland cells that produce protein hormones or enzymes.

questions.text: A student examines a cell and observes many lysosomes. What is the most likely function of this cell?
options: Breaking down waste materials and cellular debris
questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes that break down waste materials, cellular debris, and foreign invaders. Cells with many lysosomes often have roles in immune defense or cellular cleanup.

questions.text: Which sequence correctly describes the path of a protein from synthesis to secretion from the cell?
options: Ribosome → Rough ER → Golgi apparatus → Vesicle → Cell membrane
questions.explanation: Proteins destined for secretion are synthesized at ribosomes, modified in the rough ER, further processed and packaged in the Golgi, transported in vesicles to the cell membrane, and released via exocytosis.

questions.text: A cell has malfunctioning mitochondria. Which cellular process would be most directly affected?
options: Aerobic cellular respiration and ATP production
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the primary sites of aerobic cellular respiration, producing most of the cell's ATP. Malfunctioning mitochondria would significantly reduce energy production, affecting many cellular processes.

questions.text: A researcher treats a cell with a chemical that disrupts the nuclear envelope. Which cellular process would be most immediately affected?
options: Transport of mRNA from nucleus to cytoplasm for protein synthesis
questions.explanation: The nuclear envelope separates the nucleus from the cytoplasm. If disrupted, mRNA cannot properly exit the nucleus to reach ribosomes, preventing gene expression and protein synthesis. ATP production and protein synthesis at existing ribosomes could continue briefly.

questions.text: Some cells have large numbers of mitochondria while others have very few. Which type of cell would you expect to have the MOST mitochondria?
options: Cardiac muscle cells that contract continuously
Fat storage cells with large lipid droplets
Red blood cells that transport oxygen
Skin cells that form a protective barrier
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle cells require constant ATP for continuous contraction and cannot tolerate interruptions in energy supply. They contain abundant mitochondria (25-35% of cell volume) to maintain high rates of aerobic respiration.

questions.text: What type of transport moves molecules from an area of high concentration to low concentration WITHOUT using energy?
questions.explanation: Passive transport moves molecules down their concentration gradient (high to low concentration) without requiring ATP energy. Examples include diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion.

questions.text: What is the movement of WATER across a selectively permeable membrane called?
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the specific term for water movement across a selectively permeable membrane from an area of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration.

questions.text: Which process requires energy (ATP) to move molecules AGAINST their concentration gradient?
questions.explanation: Active transport uses ATP energy to move molecules against their concentration gradient (from low to high concentration). The sodium-potassium pump is a classic example.

questions.text: A red blood cell is placed in distilled water. What will happen to the cell and why?
options: The cell will swell and possibly burst because water enters by osmosis
The cell will shrink because water leaves the cell
The cell will remain unchanged because it has a cell wall
The cell will actively pump water out
questions.explanation: Distilled water is hypotonic to the red blood cell's cytoplasm. Water moves INTO the cell by osmosis, causing it to swell. Without a cell wall, red blood cells can burst (lyse) in hypotonic solutions.

questions.text: A plant cell is placed in a concentrated salt solution. What will happen to the cell?
options: The cell membrane will shrink away from the cell wall (plasmolysis)
The cell will swell and become turgid
The cell will burst due to water entering
questions.explanation: The salt solution is hypertonic. Water leaves the plant cell by osmosis, causing the central vacuole to shrink and the cell membrane to pull away from the cell wall. This process is called plasmolysis.

questions.text: A student places an Elodea leaf cell in three different solutions. In Solution A, the cell appears normal. In Solution B, the central vacuole shrinks. In Solution C, the cell becomes very turgid (swollen). Which solution is hypertonic to the cell?
questions.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the cell, causing the central vacuole to shrink. Solution B shows this effect. Solution A is isotonic (no change), and Solution C is hypotonic (water enters, causing turgidity).

questions.text: Why can't a cell maintain normal function if the sodium-potassium pump stops working?
options: The cell cannot maintain proper ion gradients essential for nerve impulses and cellular functions
questions.explanation: The sodium-potassium pump maintains the electrochemical gradient essential for nerve impulse transmission, muscle contraction, and secondary active transport. Without it, cells cannot maintain proper membrane potential or nutrient uptake.

questions.text: A researcher is studying a cell that constantly pumps hydrogen ions (H+) out of its cytoplasm. What does this indicate about the cell's environment?
options: The external environment is more acidic than the cytoplasm, and the cell uses active transport to maintain pH homeostasis
The external environment is more basic than the cytoplasm
The cell is undergoing passive transport only
H+ ions are moving by simple diffusion
questions.explanation: Pumping H+ out requires active transport (going against the gradient), indicating the external environment has a higher H+ concentration (is more acidic). Cells must expend energy to maintain internal pH homeostasis.

questions.text: A patient has a genetic disorder that impairs the function of their carrier proteins in cell membranes. Which symptom would most likely result from this disorder?
options: Inability to transport certain molecules across cell membranes, affecting nutrient absorption
Enhanced passive diffusion of all molecules
questions.explanation: Carrier proteins facilitate the transport of specific molecules (like glucose, amino acids) across membranes. Without functional carriers, cells cannot efficiently absorb these essential nutrients, leading to malnutrition and other health problems.

questions.text: Which macromolecule is the primary source of QUICK energy for living organisms?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates, especially simple sugars like glucose, provide quick energy through cellular respiration. Glucose can be rapidly metabolized to produce ATP.

questions.text: Which macromolecule provides LONG-TERM energy storage and insulation?
questions.explanation: Lipids store more energy per gram than carbohydrates and provide long-term energy storage in the form of fats. They also provide insulation and protection for organs.

questions.text: Which macromolecule is primarily responsible for growth and repair of tissues?
questions.explanation: Proteins are the building blocks of tissues. They form structural components (collagen, keratin), enzymes, hormones, and antibodies, all essential for growth and repair.

questions.text: Which macromolecule stores and transmits genetic information?
questions.explanation: Nucleic acids, specifically DNA and RNA, store and transmit genetic information. DNA contains the instructions for building all proteins, while RNA helps carry out these instructions.

questions.text: Enzymes belong to which class of macromolecules?
questions.explanation: Enzymes are proteins that act as biological catalysts. Their specific 3D structure allows them to bind to substrates and speed up chemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.text: A student tests an unknown food sample with Benedict's solution and observes a color change from blue to orange. What does this indicate?
options: The sample contains simple sugars (reducing sugars)
questions.explanation: Benedict's solution tests for reducing sugars (simple sugars like glucose and fructose). A positive test changes from blue to green, yellow, orange, or brick red depending on sugar concentration.

questions.text: A student tests a food sample with iodine and observes a color change from brown to dark blue/black. What macromolecule is present?
questions.explanation: Iodine is used to test for starch. When iodine binds to the helical structure of starch molecules, it turns dark blue or black, indicating the presence of this complex carbohydrate.

questions.text: An athlete wants to maximize glycogen storage before a marathon. Which food should they consume in the days leading up to the race?
questions.explanation: Complex carbohydrates like pasta and rice are broken down into glucose, which is stored as glycogen in muscles and liver. This glycogen serves as an energy reserve during prolonged exercise.

questions.text: Why is it important to include essential amino acids in the human diet?
options: Humans cannot synthesize these amino acids and must obtain them from food
They provide the most energy of all nutrients
They are the only amino acids used for energy
They are only found in animal products
questions.explanation: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained from dietary sources. Complete proteins contain all essential amino acids, while incomplete proteins lack one or more.

questions.text: A protein is denatured when exposed to high temperature. Which statement best explains what happened to the protein?
options: The hydrogen bonds and other weak interactions were disrupted, causing loss of the protein's 3D structure
The protein's amino acid sequence was permanently changed
The protein was broken down into individual amino acids
questions.explanation: Denaturation disrupts the weak bonds (hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions) that maintain a protein's 3D shape. The primary structure (amino acid sequence) remains intact, but the protein loses its functional shape.

questions.text: A patient has a condition where their cells cannot properly metabolize fatty acids for energy. During fasting, which symptom would most likely occur?
options: Severe fatigue and low blood sugar due to inability to access fat stores for energy
Weight gain from excess fat storage
questions.explanation: During fasting, the body normally breaks down stored fats for energy. If fatty acid metabolism is impaired, the body cannot access this energy reserve, leading to low blood sugar, fatigue, and reliance on protein breakdown for energy.

questions.text: What property of water allows it to dissolve many substances?
options: Water is a polar molecule that can form hydrogen bonds
Water is nonpolar and repels other substances
Water has a low specific heat
questions.explanation: Water's polarity allows it to form hydrogen bonds with other polar molecules, making it an excellent solvent. This is why water is called the "universal solvent" and is essential for biological processes.

questions.text: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: A pH of 7 is neutral, meaning the concentration of hydrogen ions equals the concentration of hydroxide ions. Pure water has a pH of 7 at 25°C.

questions.text: Which pH value indicates the MOST acidic solution?
questions.explanation: The pH scale ranges from 0 to 14, with lower numbers being more acidic. A pH of 2 indicates a high concentration of hydrogen ions (H+), making it strongly acidic.

questions.text: Which property of water helps organisms maintain stable body temperatures?
options: High specific heat (ability to absorb heat with little temperature change)
questions.explanation: Water's high specific heat allows it to absorb or release large amounts of energy with only small temperature changes. This helps organisms maintain stable internal temperatures despite external fluctuations.

questions.text: A solution has a pH of 3. How does its hydrogen ion concentration compare to a solution with a pH of 6?
options: The pH 3 solution has 1,000 times more hydrogen ions than the pH 6 solution
The pH 3 solution has 3 times more hydrogen ions
The pH 3 solution has fewer hydrogen ions
Both solutions have the same hydrogen ion concentration
questions.explanation: Each pH unit represents a 10-fold difference in hydrogen ion concentration. From pH 6 to pH 3 is a difference of 3 units, meaning 10³ = 1,000 times more H+ ions at pH 3.

questions.text: Why are buffers important in biological systems?
options: Buffers help maintain stable pH by resisting changes when acids or bases are added
Buffers increase the pH of solutions
questions.explanation: Buffers are solutions that resist changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added. They are crucial in biological systems to maintain the narrow pH ranges required for enzyme function and cellular processes.

questions.text: A lake has become acidified due to acid rain, with the pH dropping from 7 to 5. Which organism would be most affected?
options: Fish species that require a narrow pH range for survival
All organisms would be equally affected
questions.explanation: Many aquatic organisms, especially fish, are highly sensitive to pH changes. A drop from pH 7 to pH 5 represents a 100-fold increase in acidity, which can disrupt enzyme function, gill function, and reproduction in sensitive species.

questions.text: Blood has a pH of approximately 7.4. If a patient's blood pH drops to 7.1, which statement best describes this condition?
options: The patient has acidosis, a potentially life-threatening condition where blood is too acidic
The patient has alkalosis, where blood is too basic
The change is insignificant and within normal range
The patient's blood pH has increased
questions.explanation: Normal blood pH is 7.35-7.45. A pH of 7.1 is below this range and indicates acidosis. Even small changes in blood pH can be dangerous because enzymes and cellular processes require very specific pH ranges.

questions.text: In Mendel's experiments, what did he call the trait that appeared in the first generation (F1) when crossing two purebred plants?
questions.explanation: Mendel called the trait that appeared in the F1 generation "dominant" because it masked the expression of the other trait, which he called "recessive." This became the foundation of Mendelian genetics.

questions.text: What is a Punnett square used for?
options: To predict the possible genotypes of offspring from a genetic cross
To determine the age of an organism
questions.explanation: A Punnett square is a diagram used to predict the genetic outcomes of a cross. It shows all possible combinations of alleles from the parents and their probability in the offspring.

questions.text: If an organism has two identical alleles for a trait (e.g., BB or bb), what is its genotype called?
questions.explanation: When an organism has two identical alleles for a gene (BB or bb), it is homozygous for that trait. If it has two different alleles (Bb), it is heterozygous.

questions.text: In pea plants, tall (T) is dominant over short (t). If two heterozygous tall plants (Tt) are crossed, what percentage of the offspring will be short?
questions.explanation: Crossing Tt × Tt produces genotypes: 25% TT, 50% Tt, and 25% tt. Only the tt genotype (25%) results in short plants because short is recessive.

questions.text: In guinea pigs, black fur (B) is dominant over white fur (b). If a black guinea pig mates with a white guinea pig and produces both black and white offspring, what is the genotype of the black parent?
questions.explanation: Since some offspring are white (bb), they must have received a recessive allele from each parent. This means the black parent must carry a recessive allele (Bb). A BB parent could only produce black offspring.

questions.text: In humans, attached earlobes are recessive (e) and free earlobes are dominant (E). A man with attached earlobes marries a woman with free earlobes. They have a child with attached earlobes. What is the mother's genotype?
questions.explanation: The father must be ee (attached earlobes). For the child to have attached earlobes (ee), the mother must have contributed a recessive allele. Since she has free earlobes, she must be Ee (heterozygous).

questions.text: In a certain plant, flower color is determined by incomplete dominance. Red (RR) and white (WW) flowers produce pink (RW) offspring. If two pink-flowered plants are crossed, what is the expected phenotypic ratio?
questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, crossing RW × RW produces 25% RR (red), 50% RW (pink), and 25% WW (white). The phenotypic ratio is 1:2:1 because heterozygotes show an intermediate phenotype.

questions.text: In humans, blood type is determined by multiple alleles (IA, IB, i). A person with type A blood and a person with type B blood have a child with type O blood. Which statement about the parents' genotypes is correct?
options: Both parents must be heterozygous (IAi and IBi)
This is impossible; type O cannot come from these parents
questions.explanation: For a child to have type O blood (ii), both parents must contribute an i allele. Since both parents have A and B blood types, they must both be heterozygous: IAi (type A) and IBi (type B).

questions.text: What is the shape of a DNA molecule called?
questions.explanation: DNA has a double helix shape, resembling a twisted ladder. The two strands run in opposite directions (antiparallel) and are held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs.

questions.text: Which bases pair together in DNA?
options: Adenine pairs with Thymine; Guanine pairs with Cytosine
Adenine pairs with Guanine; Thymine pairs with Cytosine
All bases can pair with any other base
Adenine pairs with Cytosine; Guanine pairs with Thymine
questions.explanation: In DNA, complementary base pairing follows the rule: A-T and G-C. Adenine always pairs with Thymine (2 hydrogen bonds), and Guanine always pairs with Cytosine (3 hydrogen bonds).

questions.text: What is the function of DNA replication?
options: To make an exact copy of DNA before cell division
questions.explanation: DNA replication creates an identical copy of the DNA molecule before a cell divides. This ensures that each daughter cell receives a complete set of genetic instructions.

questions.text: During DNA replication, what is the role of DNA polymerase?
options: To add nucleotides to the growing DNA strand in the 5' to 3' direction
To break hydrogen bonds between the two DNA strands
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase is the primary enzyme that adds complementary nucleotides to the growing DNA strand. It can only add nucleotides in the 5' to 3' direction and proofreads for errors.

questions.text: Why is DNA replication described as "semi-conservative"?
options: Each new DNA molecule contains one original strand and one newly synthesized strand
Half of the DNA is conserved and half is destroyed
Each new DNA molecule contains two completely new strands
questions.explanation: Semi-conservative replication means that each daughter DNA molecule contains one strand from the original (parent) molecule and one newly synthesized strand. This was confirmed by the Meselson-Stahl experiment.

questions.text: On one strand of DNA, the sequence is 5'-ATGCGA-3'. What is the complementary sequence on the other strand?
questions.explanation: DNA strands are complementary and antiparallel. A pairs with T, T pairs with A, G pairs with C, C pairs with G. The complementary strand runs in the opposite direction (3' to 5').

questions.text: During DNA replication, the leading strand is synthesized continuously, while the lagging strand is synthesized in fragments called Okazaki fragments. Why is this necessary?
options: DNA polymerase can only synthesize DNA in the 5' to 3' direction, so the lagging strand must be made in short segments
The lagging strand contains more errors
The leading strand is more important
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides in the 5' to 3' direction. Since the two DNA strands run antiparallel, the lagging strand must be synthesized in short segments (Okazaki fragments) that are later joined together.

questions.text: What is the purpose of meiosis?
options: To produce gametes (sex cells) with half the chromosome number
questions.explanation: Meiosis is a specialized cell division that produces gametes (sperm and egg cells) with half the normal chromosome number. This ensures that when fertilization occurs, the offspring have the correct chromosome number.

questions.text: How many divisions occur in meiosis?
options: Two divisions (Meiosis I and Meiosis II)
questions.explanation: Meiosis consists of two consecutive divisions: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes, and Meiosis II separates sister chromatids. This produces four haploid cells from one diploid cell.

questions.text: In humans, how many chromosomes are in a typical body cell versus a gamete?
options: 46 chromosomes in body cells; 23 in gametes
questions.explanation: Human body (somatic) cells are diploid with 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Gametes are haploid with 23 chromosomes (one from each pair), produced by meiosis.

questions.text: Crossing over occurs during which phase of meiosis?
questions.explanation: Crossing over (exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes) occurs during Prophase I of meiosis. This process creates new combinations of alleles and increases genetic variation.

questions.text: How does meiosis contribute to genetic variation in offspring?
options: Through crossing over, independent assortment, and random fertilization
questions.explanation: Meiosis creates genetic variation through: (1) crossing over during Prophase I, (2) independent assortment of chromosomes during Metaphase I, and (3) random fertilization of gametes.

questions.text: A cell has 10 chromosomes (5 pairs). After meiosis, how many chromosomes will be in each gamete?
questions.explanation: Meiosis reduces the chromosome number by half. If the parent cell has 10 chromosomes (diploid), each gamete will have 5 chromosomes (haploid).

questions.text: Nondisjunction occurs when homologous chromosomes fail to separate properly during meiosis. What condition results if nondisjunction of chromosome 21 occurs during meiosis I?
questions.explanation: Nondisjunction of chromosome 21 results in gametes with an extra chromosome 21. When fertilized, the resulting zygote has three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21), causing Down syndrome.

questions.text: What is natural selection?
options: The process where organisms with favorable traits survive and reproduce more successfully
Random selection of organisms to survive
Human breeding of organisms for desired traits
The elimination of all weak organisms
questions.explanation: Natural selection is the mechanism of evolution proposed by Darwin. Organisms with traits better suited to their environment survive at higher rates and pass those advantageous traits to their offspring.

questions.text: Which type of evidence supports the theory of evolution?
options: Fossil record, comparative anatomy, and molecular biology
questions.explanation: Multiple lines of evidence support evolution: the fossil record shows change over time, comparative anatomy reveals homologous structures, and molecular biology shows genetic similarities between related species.

questions.text: What is an adaptation?
options: A trait that improves an organism's ability to survive and reproduce in its environment
A trait that makes organisms bigger
questions.explanation: An adaptation is a heritable trait that increases an organism's fitness (ability to survive and reproduce) in a particular environment. Adaptations arise through natural selection.

questions.text: A population of beetles lives on a dark-colored tree trunk. Dark-colored beetles are more common than light-colored beetles. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Dark beetles are better camouflaged from predators, giving them higher survival rates
The environment randomly favors dark beetles
questions.explanation: This is an example of natural selection. Dark beetles are camouflaged on dark bark, so fewer are eaten by predators. They survive and reproduce more successfully, making the dark trait more common in the population.

questions.text: Fossil evidence shows that whales evolved from land-dwelling mammals. Which evidence would best support this transition?
options: Fossils showing gradual changes from land mammals with legs to whales with flippers
Whales appeared suddenly in the fossil record
questions.explanation: Transitional fossils show intermediate forms between ancestors and descendants. Whale evolution is well-documented with fossils showing gradual changes: from walking mammals to amphibious forms to fully aquatic whales.

questions.text: Two species of desert mice live in different geographic regions but have similar adaptations for desert survival. What type of evolution does this represent?
questions.explanation: Convergent evolution occurs when unrelated species evolve similar traits due to similar environmental pressures. Desert mice in different regions evolved similar adaptations independently because they face similar challenges.

questions.text: A population of insects is exposed to a pesticide. After several generations, most insects are resistant to the pesticide. Which statement best explains this?
options: Insects with pre-existing resistance genes survived and reproduced, passing the trait to offspring
The pesticide caused mutations in all insects
questions.explanation: Natural selection: some insects had random mutations providing resistance. When the pesticide was applied, resistant insects survived and reproduced, while susceptible ones died. Over generations, resistance became common in the population.

questions.text: Which level of classification contains the MOST different types of organisms?
questions.explanation: Kingdom is the broadest and most inclusive taxonomic level shown, containing the greatest diversity of organisms. Species is the most specific level, containing only organisms that can interbreed.

questions.text: What is binomial nomenclature?
options: A two-word naming system using genus and species
A naming system using only the family name
A system that names all organisms the same
questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature, developed by Linnaeus, gives each organism a two-part scientific name: genus (capitalized) and species (lowercase). For example, Homo sapiens (humans).

questions.text: Humans belong to which domain?
questions.explanation: Humans are eukaryotes (cells with a nucleus) and belong to Domain Eukarya. This domain includes all organisms with eukaryotic cells: animals, plants, fungi, and protists.

questions.text: A cladogram shows evolutionary relationships between species. What does a branch point (node) represent?
options: A common ancestor shared by the species on the branches above it
questions.explanation: Branch points (nodes) on a cladogram represent common ancestors. Each branch point shows where two lineages diverged from a shared ancestor, indicated by a shared derived characteristic.

questions.text: Which organisms would be MOST closely related based on their scientific names?
options: Canis latrans and Canis lupus (same genus, different species)
Felis catus and Canis familiaris (different genera)
Panthera leo and Felis catus (same family, different genera)
questions.explanation: Organisms in the same genus are more closely related than those in different genera. Canis latrans (coyote) and Canis lupus (wolf) share a more recent common ancestor than organisms in different genera.

questions.text: Scientists find a new organism. It has cell walls made of chitin and is heterotrophic. To which kingdom does it most likely belong?
questions.explanation: Organisms with cell walls made of chitin and that are heterotrophic (cannot make their own food) are classified as fungi. Plants have cellulose cell walls and are autotrophic; animals lack cell walls.

questions.text: What is symbiosis?
options: A close, long-term relationship between two different species
questions.explanation: Symbiosis is a close, long-term biological interaction between two different species. The three main types are mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism.

questions.text: In mutualism, how do the interacting species benefit?
questions.explanation: Mutualism is a symbiotic relationship where both species benefit. Examples include bees and flowers (bees get food, flowers get pollination) and clownfish and sea anemones.

questions.text: A tick feeds on a dog's blood, harming the dog. What type of symbiotic relationship is this?
questions.explanation: Parasitism is a relationship where one organism (the parasite, like a tick) benefits while the other (the host, like the dog) is harmed. The parasite takes nutrients from the host.

questions.text: Clownfish live inside sea anemones. The clownfish is protected by the anemone's stinging tentacles (which don't harm it), and the clownfish attracts prey for the anemone. What type of symbiosis is this?
questions.explanation: This is mutualism: the clownfish gets protection from predators, and the anemone gets prey attracted by the clownfish. Both organisms benefit from the relationship.

questions.text: Barnacles attach to a whale's skin. The barnacles get a place to live and access to food, while the whale is not affected. What type of symbiosis is this?
questions.explanation: Commensalism is a relationship where one organism benefits and the other is unaffected. The barnacles benefit, but the whale neither benefits nor is harmed significantly.

questions.text: Why are keystone species important in ecosystems?
options: They have a disproportionately large effect on ecosystem structure relative to their abundance
They are the most common species
questions.explanation: Keystone species have a large impact on ecosystem structure despite often being relatively rare. Removing a keystone species can cause dramatic ecosystem changes, like the collapse of entire food webs.

questions.text: Sea otters are keystone species in Pacific kelp forests. They eat sea urchins, which eat kelp. What would happen if sea otters were removed from this ecosystem?
options: Sea urchins would overpopulate and destroy kelp forests, reducing biodiversity
More species would move into the area
questions.explanation: Without sea otters, sea urchin populations would explode. Urchins eat kelp, so overpopulation would destroy kelp forests. This would eliminate habitat for many species, reducing overall biodiversity.

questions.text: In a food chain, which organism is typically a producer?
questions.explanation: Producers are autotrophs that make their own food through photosynthesis. Plants like grass convert sunlight into chemical energy stored in glucose, forming the base of most food chains.

questions.text: What is a consumer that eats both plants and animals called?
questions.explanation: Omnivores eat both plants and animals. Examples include humans, bears, and pigs. Herbivores eat only plants, carnivores eat only animals, and decomposers break down dead organic matter.

questions.text: Which organism breaks down dead organisms and returns nutrients to the ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Decomposers like bacteria and fungi break down dead organisms and waste products, returning nutrients like carbon and nitrogen to the soil where they can be used by producers.

questions.text: In the food chain: grass → rabbit → snake → hawk, which organism is a secondary consumer?
questions.explanation: Secondary consumers eat primary consumers. Grass is the producer, rabbit is the primary consumer (eats grass), snake is the secondary consumer (eats rabbit), and hawk is the tertiary consumer.

questions.text: Approximately how much energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next in an ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Only about 10% of energy is transferred between trophic levels. The other 90% is lost as heat, used for metabolic processes, or not consumed. This limits the length of food chains.

questions.text: Why are there usually fewer organisms at higher trophic levels (like top predators)?
options: Less energy is available at higher trophic levels due to energy loss between levels
There are always more predators than prey
questions.explanation: Energy decreases at each trophic level (10% rule). With less energy available, fewer organisms can be supported. This is why there are fewer wolves than deer, and fewer deer than plants.

questions.text: A food web contains: grass, rabbits, mice, snakes, hawks, and foxes. If all snakes are removed from the ecosystem, what is the most likely immediate effect?
options: Rabbit and mouse populations would increase; hawk and fox populations would decrease
All populations would stay the same
questions.explanation: Snakes are predators of mice and rabbits. Removing snakes removes predation pressure, so prey populations increase. Hawks lose a food source, potentially decreasing. Foxes might eat more mice, but overall ecosystem balance is disrupted.

questions.text: Which process in the water cycle involves water vapor cooling and turning into liquid water?
questions.explanation: Condensation is when water vapor (gas) cools and changes into liquid water droplets. This process forms clouds. Evaporation is the opposite—liquid to gas.

questions.text: Which gas do plants absorb from the atmosphere during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: During photosynthesis, plants absorb carbon dioxide (CO₂) from the atmosphere and use the carbon to build glucose. They release oxygen as a byproduct.

questions.text: What process returns carbon dioxide to the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration by organisms releases CO₂ when glucose is broken down for energy. Decomposition by bacteria and fungi also releases CO₂ from dead organic matter.

questions.text: Why are bacteria essential to the nitrogen cycle?
options: They convert nitrogen gas (N₂) into forms plants can use through nitrogen fixation
Plants can use atmospheric nitrogen directly
questions.explanation: Most organisms cannot use atmospheric nitrogen (N₂). Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert N₂ into ammonia and other compounds that plants can absorb. This makes nitrogen available to the food web.

questions.text: Fossil fuels are formed from ancient organisms. How does burning fossil fuels affect the carbon cycle?
options: It releases stored carbon back into the atmosphere as CO₂, increasing atmospheric CO₂ levels
It removes CO₂ from the atmosphere
It has no effect on CO₂ levels
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels contain carbon that was stored underground for millions of years. Burning them releases this stored carbon as CO₂, increasing atmospheric CO₂ faster than natural processes can remove it.

questions.text: A lake receives excess nutrients from agricultural runoff. This causes an algal bloom, followed by algae death and bacterial decomposition. What effect does this have on the lake?
options: Bacteria consume oxygen during decomposition, potentially creating a "dead zone" where fish cannot survive
Oxygen levels increase, benefiting all organisms
questions.explanation: Eutrophication: excess nutrients cause algae overgrowth. When algae die, bacteria decompose them using large amounts of oxygen. This can create hypoxic (low oxygen) or anoxic (no oxygen) conditions, killing fish and other aquatic organisms.

questions.text: What is an invasive species?
options: A non-native species that spreads rapidly and harms the ecosystem
A species that is native to an area
A species that helps the ecosystem
questions.explanation: Invasive species are organisms introduced to a new area where they have no natural predators or competitors. They spread rapidly, outcompeting native species and disrupting ecosystems.

questions.text: Which human activity contributes MOST to increased atmospheric carbon dioxide?
options: Burning fossil fuels for energy and transportation
questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) releases CO₂ that was stored underground for millions of years. This is the largest human contribution to rising atmospheric CO₂ levels.

questions.text: What is biodiversity?
options: The variety of life in an ecosystem or across Earth
The number of only endangered species
questions.explanation: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life at all levels: genetic diversity within species, species diversity within ecosystems, and ecosystem diversity across regions. Higher biodiversity generally means healthier ecosystems.

questions.text: Why are invasive species often successful in new environments?
options: They lack natural predators and competitors in their new environment
They are always stronger than native species
questions.explanation: Invasive species succeed because they escape the predators, parasites, and competitors that controlled their populations in their native range. Without these checks, they can grow unchecked and outcompete native species.

questions.text: Which practice would BEST help conserve biodiversity?
options: Protecting habitats and creating wildlife corridors between fragmented areas
questions.explanation: Habitat protection and creating connections between habitat fragments help species survive by maintaining large enough populations and allowing migration and genetic exchange.

questions.text: A region experiences deforestation, removing 80% of its forest cover. Which long-term effect would be most likely?
options: Reduced biodiversity, disrupted water cycles, increased soil erosion, and altered local climate
Increased biodiversity as new species move in
questions.explanation: Deforestation has cascading effects: loss of habitat reduces biodiversity; without trees, transpiration decreases, altering local rainfall; roots no longer hold soil, causing erosion; and carbon storage is reduced, contributing to climate change.
